issue_title: Very unhelpful startup traceback if the syslog is unavailable
issue_body:
If for some reason the system logging socket is unavailable (i.e. the syslog failed to start, or is not installed) all of the daemons and init tasks provided by this package die with a traceback for a socket error.  The errno returned is 2 (no such file or directory).

It would be nice to catch that exception when the logging is starting up and spit out a print statement that the logging socket is unavailable.  It would also be nice to have an option to gracefully degrade rather than just hitting a hard fault.

issue_title: Adding Metadata to an existing ec2.Instance instance generates an AttributeError
issue_body:
Using the [example](https://github.com/cloudtools/troposphere/blob/master/examples/CloudFormation_Init_ConfigSet.py) it is possible to set the Metadata for an ec2 instance when it is initialised. However you get an exception if you try to define the Metadata property after the instance has been initialised, for e.g.

```Python
myInstance = ec2.Instance('MyEC2Instance')
myInstance.Metadata = cloudformation.Metadata(...)
``` 
generates the following error:
`AttributeError: AWS::EC2::Instance object does not support attribute Metadata`

All the other parameters in the example (i.e. UserData, KeyName, ImageId, InstanceType, etc) can be set on an existing instance object. 

Is there a reason why setting the Metadata property is not supported in this way? I can set the same Metadata against the Template without error.

issue_title: DependsOn doesn't play well with lists of dependencies
issue_body:
```python
DependsOn=[Vpc, RouteTable]
```

successfully creates a template. But cloudformation will fail when you try to use it.

> Template format error: Every DependsOn value must be a string.

work around: 
```python
DependsOn=[Vpc.title, RouteTable.title]
```
or
```python
from troposphere import depends_on_helper
DependsOn=map(depends_on_helper, [Vpc, RouteTable])
```

`depends_on_helper` should probably be updated to handle lists.

issue_title: setup_py broken on 1.9.0rc1
issue_body:
## Issue

Running `pants setup-py <target>` produces content that is not installable by python3 virtualenvs.

## Reproducing

I have a full example in [this repository](https://github.com/alexcouper/pants-setup-py-issue)

```
./pants setup-py python/my-library::
```

And then from a python3 virtualenv try to install the generated package.

```
(python3) $ pip install dist/Mylibrary-0.1.0.tar.gz
Processing ./dist/Mylibrary-0.1.0.tar.gz
    Complete output from command python setup.py egg_info:
    error in Mylibrary setup command: 'install_requires' must be a string or list of strings containing valid project/version requirement specifiers; 'int' object is not iterable
```

My python3 virtualenv is running `3.6.5`

## Debugging

It seems on 1.8, the setup.py file looked like this:
```

# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE -- AUTOGENERATED BY PANTS
# Target: PythonLibrary(BuildFileAddress(python/mylibrary/BUILD, Mylibrary))

from setuptools import setup

setup(**
{   'description': 'Python library.',
    'install_requires': [ 'requests==2.18.4', 'pika==0.11.2', 'boto3==1.7.50'],
    'name': 'Mylibrary',
    'version': '0.1.0',
    'zip_safe': True}
)
```

On 1.9.0.rc1 we see this instead:
```

# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE -- AUTOGENERATED BY PANTS
# Target: PythonLibrary(BuildFileAddress(python/mylibrary/BUILD, Mylibrary))

from setuptools import setup

setup(**
{   'description': 'Python library.',
    'install_requires': [   b'requests==2.18.4',
                            b'pika==0.11.2',
                            b'boto3==1.7.50'],
    'name': 'Mylibrary',
    'version': '0.1.0',
    'zip_safe': True}
)
```

issue_title: Ivy product dependees with custom conf requirements should call ivy themselves.
issue_body:
Currently there is a hack in `IvyResolve` that uses the fake products 'jar_map_default', 'jar_map_sources', and 'jar_map_javadoc' as signals to resolve using extra confs.  These signals are currently used by the `IdeGen` and `Export` tasks.  Just have these tasks mix in IvyTaskMixin or use IvyUtils and do their own resolves.

This idea was discussed in https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/716/

issue_title: Investigate content addressable storage options
issue_body:
Still part of #2870, #2966 is going to make all products content addressable, which needs to be read/written by multiprocess/remote workers. Having a local/remote db storing and provide access to those artifacts would not only be more scalable but also simplifies the communication between engine and workers (#2965)

A few requirements:
* storing blobs
* secondary index support
* good multiprocessing support (pants already uses `sqlite`, but we often see issues like "database is locked")

Long term will involve better fs/scm support which is underway, so this is for short-mid term

issue_title: Port `visualizer` out of `examples` and into the Scheduler
issue_body:
Currently the `visualizer` is a unique entrypoint for the `examples` package. Consequently, we can only visualize executions that use the tasks defined in the examples package.

Instead, the "meat" of visualization should move out of https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/engine/exp/examples/visualizer.py and into https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/engine/exp/scheduler.py (or some other module in the root `exp` package), where it can be executed at any time by an interested caller. The `visualizer` module would then use the logic in the core package to visualize things.

As a reminder: the visualizer is currently executed like:
```
./pants run src/python/pants/engine/exp/examples:viz --   .   list  3rdparty/::
```

issue_title: JVM deprecation warnings on update
issue_body:
I am upgrading pants right now and I've run into hundreds of these deprecation warnings for every `BUILD` file.
```
[WARN] .cache/pants/setup/bootstrap-Darwin-x86_64/1.4.0.dev16/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pants/backend/jvm/targets/exportable_jvm_library.py:23: DeprecationWarning: DEPRECATED: The `resources=` JVM target argument found on target src/jvm/com/thesigma/bag:bag will be removed in version 1.5.0.dev0.
  Use `dependencies=` instead.
  super(ExportableJvmLibrary, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
```

Our `BUILD` files are currently modeled something like this:
```
scala_library(
  name = 'service',
  dependencies = [
    '3rdparty/jvm/io/finch',
  ],
  resources = [],
  sources = rglobs('*.scala'),
)

```

So changing `resources` to `dependencies` breaks. Did the original `dependencies` get renamed to something else, and `resources` to `dependencies`?

issue_title: Missing dependency hides circular dependency
issue_body:
While working on #4136 I noticed the following circular dependency:
```
Exception message: Cycle detected:
	src/python/pants/backend/jvm/targets:jvm ->
	src/python/pants/backend/jvm/subsystems:junit ->
	src/python/pants/backend/jvm/subsystems:shader ->
	src/python/pants/backend/jvm/targets:jvm
```
The corresponding class level cycle is `JUnitTests` -> `Junit` -> `Shader` -> `JarDependency`

It didn't break is only because the `targets:jvm` to `subsystems:junit` dependency is currently missing, which actually exists (`junit_tests.py` needs `JUnit`). As soon as we add the missing dependency, it will break.
```
diff --git a/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/targets/BUILD b/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/targets/BUILD
index eee27ff..e7bbe16 100644
--- a/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/targets/BUILD
+++ b/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/targets/BUILD
@@ -33,6 +33,7 @@ python_library(
     '3rdparty/python/twitter/commons:twitter.common.dirutil',
     '3rdparty/python:six',
     'src/python/pants/backend/jvm/subsystems:java',
+    'src/python/pants/backend/jvm/subsystems:junit',
     'src/python/pants/backend/jvm/subsystems:jvm_platform',
     'src/python/pants/backend/jvm:jar_dependency_utils',
     'src/python/pants/base:build_environment',
```

We might need to break `src/python/pants/backend/jvm/targets:jvm` into smaller ones.

issue_title: The setup_repo docs describe setting up source_roots but neglect registering bootstrap BUILD files
Can't handle non-ASCII characters
issue_body:
The module is not able to handle input with non-ASCII characters. If unicode is passed, the str() conversion on most of the parameters throws UnicodeEncodeErrors as the Python default encoding of ASCII is not able to handle the non-ASCII characters.

If UTF-8 encoded strings are passed, etree's element.tostring() method is expecting unicode and tries to encode it:

      File "C:\python27\lib\site-packages\junit_xml\__init__.py", line 169, in to_xml_string
        xml_string = ET.tostring(xml_element, encoding=encoding)

issue_title: Plugin system wishlist
issue_body:
I propose we iterate on the plugin system by creating successively more elaborate examples. [Here are some motivating examples.](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1s8kqh1NSbHlzPCtaI1AbIsLsgGH3bopYbZdM1RzgxN8/edit#heading=h.peealb65mrfb)

As I understand it we want a plugin system that does the following:
- [ ] Allow plugins to insert their own javascript in existing pages, for instance to view content or add a new coach report type.
- [ ] Allow plugins to define new views and urls.
- [ ] Allow plugins to override base templates (for a custom look-and-feel).
- [ ] Has good documentation with examples for all features.
- [ ] Has documentation of core hooks.

Additionally I'm guessing we want the following features:
- [ ] Plugin mixins to cut down on boilerplate. For example, we might have a NavItemPluginMixin that can be thrown in to painlessly add new nav menu items by simply implementing one function.
- [ ] Easily allow an app to register multiple callbacks to a hook.
- [ ] Allows users to define new hooks.

What do you all think?

issue_title: Make dedupFasta work on our Reads class
Separate the alignment panel and HTML generation in graphics.py
issue_body:
In `graphics.py` the `alignmentPanel` function does two things: makes a panel of blue plots and makes individual blue plot images and HTML to display them all. The first of these can be very memory intensive - so much so that it becomes hard to display even a few blue plots if they have a ton of reads. It's also slow. And the two things are used in different contexts: the panel in iPython notebook and the images + HTML viewed in a browser, stored on a server, etc.

So.... separate the two. The code gets simpler, faster, and we can make large "panels" in HTML using a table, including having full titles that wrap and having images linked to their full-sized files. We can then also have the code run on larger result sets. It's a much better way to do things.

issue_title: How to share session between saspy and cell magics?
issue_body:
This library is enormously useful for cases when I want to write in Python but switch to SAS for certain heavy lifting segments -- but I'm running into one workflow issue when attempting to combine the object-based saspy methods with the cell magics approach.

My goal is to do something like this, in a series of cells in one notebook:

```python
# start a session
import saspy
import pandas as pd
from IPython.display import HTML

sas = saspy.SASsession(cfgname='ssh')
```

 Later, load something into the SAS session:

```python
sas_dataset = sas.df2sd(something_in_pandas, 'mydata')
```

Now, instantiate cell magics so that I can submit SAS statements directly:

```python
%load_ext saspy.sas_magic
```

Do something useful in SAS (hello world example here):

```python
%%SAS

proc print data=WORK.mydata; run;
```

But the issue is that the cell magics extension, when loaded, creates a new SAS session on the other end, separate from the one created with the direct `saspy.SASsession` call, which means that my work library and other instantiated data are not available to me in any %SAS cells.

I can of course get around this by using the more explicit syntax:

```python
c = sas.submit("""
  ... sas code here
""")
HTML(c['LST'])
```

But that isn't quite as elegant for a single notebook trading off tasks between python and SAS. Is there a way to force a shared session between both, or a reason to prevent that?  

Ideally, an existing session could simply be passed through into the magics when loaded. This line is where the new session is created:

https://github.com/sassoftware/saspy/blob/master/saspy/sas_magic.py#L36

Although as far as I can tell from API docs, the `load_ext` call in iPython doesn't allow arguments, so I'm not sure how passing in an already instantiated SAS session would be done in this case.

issue_title: PenaltyModel when loading from an unlike BQM should do the conversion
Big batch requests fail with "413 Payload Too Large"
issue_body:
Server-side body size limit for problem submit endpoint is too low to accept 100 (the client default) full problems (all biases and all couplings set).

The solution: change batching stop criteria from number of problems to total size.

issue_title: Marshalling of int-like objects not inherited from int is broken
issue_body: Consider `numpy.int64`. If we simply add it via `Writer.register(numpy.int64, IntHandler)`, it won't work since  `Marshaller.emit_int` explicitly checks `isinstance(rep, int)` and if the check returns False, `rep` is passed as-is, without calling `str`.

issue_title: question: when duplicate kerning is present, could we utilize not a random strategy?
issue_body:
Why wouldn't we use the following rule (I just made it up, so this might be just an uneducated/novice question :-)):
'When duplicate kerning rules found for a glyph pair then we use a rule that gets characters "closest" of the two rules if signs indicate this is "closer kerning", "furthest" otherwise'
when resolving the conflicts?

My "novice" thinking to justify this:
- kerning is mostly (all the time?) used to get characters "closer" 
- duplicate kerning is either a sign of incomplete (or not-fully debugged) design or maybe a conscious design decision (I have no idea about it)
- we could resolve the conflict in one of the following ways:
 a. randomly (that's what it looks to me right now)
if both signs of numbers are the same then [I assume signs are about getting closer or further]:
  b. pick a rule that gets characters "furtherest" of the two if signs indicate this is "further kerning"
  c. pick a rule that gets characters "closest" of the two if signs indicate this is "closer kerning"
if signs are different then to me it indicates that the designer is very confused and we should do a. (random) or 
 d. pick an average between the two numbers :-)


Just an idea/question from a novice in the "font business."

the list below is just a fragment from processing Ethiopic glyphs with the highest absolute number of the pair in bold

issue_title: task change event with nimi nidcpower sequence sweep
issue_body:
Hi,
I have the following use case. I define a sequence on a nidcpower device using the nimi nidcpower lib. during this sequence I need to observe if a digital input line changes its state. If the state changes I need to measure the nidcpower voltage.

Problem is, that the implementation of `wait_for_event(nidcpower.Event.SEQUENCE_ENGINE_DONE, 10)` is obviously blocking. Means that my callbacks for the registered events are lost. At least I do not enter the callback at all. So I refactored the implementation to use a thread for both, the nidaqmx task, and nidcpower `wait_for_event(nidcpower.Event.SEQUENCE_ENGINE_DONE, 10)` . Unfortunately the callback is still not invoked. Is this because the sync option in the c_func call for register_signal_event is always '0'? compare this help site
[http://zone.ni.com/reference/en-XX/help/370471AE-01/daqmxcfunc/daqmxregistersignalevent/](url)

If this is the issue, what would be the correct value for this option to be able to invoke the callback from within the thread?

Or is my use case not supported?

issue_title: Allow for custom logger to be passed to ApplicationSession
issue_body:
ApplicationSession does not currently allow a custom logger to be passed to the logger instance that txaio uses. It would be nice to allow a custom logger to be passed based on the standard lib logging module. This would allow for better control of a log file for administration, and not duplicate entry with no way to stop it:

``` text
[04-28-2016 15:58:32.589 UTC] PID: 1659 Mod: timer Line: 16 INFO: build_json_tree sort finished in: 0.002349 secs
2016-04-28T11:58:32 build_json_tree sort finished in: 0.002349 secs
``` 

``` python
class ApplicationSession(BaseSession):
    """
    WAMP endpoint session.
    """

    log = txaio.make_logger() or self.config.logger  # NOTE: Or something like this

    def __init__(self, config=None):
        """
        Constructor.
        """
        BaseSession.__init__(self)
        self.config = config or types.ComponentConfig(realm=u"default")

``` python

issue_title: Document non-determinism on the website and/or in the docs
issue_body:
There is significant variance in training runs due to CUDNN.  We can't really fix this, but it should at least be clearly documented wherever it is relevant.

Here's some analysis done by @OyvindTafjord, pasted here from a private email, so we have somewhere public to point to if this comes up again, and some notes for anyone wanting to make a PR with documentation.

----------------

I think I isolated the issue causing our BiDAF on SQuAD training runs to produce different results in spite of fixed random seeds (I have seen 67.7, 67.9, and 68.3 ExactMatch in three different runs, Matt G got 68.7 in another one). It seems to be related to inherent indeterminism in CUDNN code, due to the way things are optimized for speed. As I understand it, asynchronous calculations can cause operations to be done in different orders, so numerical accuracy causes differences which then magnify (to surprising amount?) in big computations. 

The upshot is that there doesn't seem to be a way to avoid this without taking a significant speed penalty (using CUNN instead of CUDNN for GPU computations), but other than causing annoying noise in experiments, there probably isn't much reason to demand such determinism. E.g., although the results are reproducible using CUNN or CPU, they are different between CUNN and CPU (and also between different CUDA versions and CPU OS'es). Rather one should presumably just treat this as actual experimental noise and perhaps try to estimate the uncertainty this gives to the results.

Here are some random mentions of this:

https://github.com/soumith/cudnn.torch/issues/274
https://discuss.pytorch.org/t/choose-backends/271
https://discuss.pytorch.org/t/non-determinisic-results/459/4 
https://github.com/soumith/cudnn.torch/issues/270

To confirm that this is what's going on, I ran a few configurations of BiDAF on a 10% sample of SQuAD (on an AWS p2.xlarge):

1) Default  (CUDNN)
2) Set torch.backends.cudnn.enabled = False so that CUNN is used instead of CUDNN
3) CPU mode  ("cuda_device": -1)

In CUDNN mode, the results are indeed varying between runs, even after one epoch. E.g, after 2 epochs I see validation F1/loss of 15.8/7.20 vs 16.1/7.17 in two different runs. In CUNN mode, the results were identical in two runs (20.8/6.85). Training in CPU mode also gives same results in each run, but not the same as CUNN (19.0/6.99). I got yet different numbers in CPU mode on my laptop (19.6/6.94). Given this, consistency between runs in a particular mode/computer seems like a somewhat arbitrary requirement.

Of course, CUNN is significantly slower than CUDNN although it's still using the GPU. For this example, an epoch took:

1) CUDNN: 3.6 minutes
2) CUNN: 10 minutes
3) CPU: 63/85 minutes (p2.xlarge/my laptop)

Training on full SQuAD takes about 105 minutes per epoch for CUNN (compared to 38 minutes on CUDNN), so about 2.8x slowdown in both cases. FWIW, a CUNN run on the full SQuAD reached EM/F1 67.8/77.4 after epoch 6 (http://beaker.allenai.org/ex/ex_h2ujgatwzgjl, still in progress at epoch 10). I also noticed that even CUNN runs varied between my p2 machine and running in beaker using the docker image, I'm guessing due to different CUDA versions (7.5 vs 8). I did confirm that two separate beaker runs agreed after 2 epochs.

issue_title: memory_docker.py doesn't log errors connecting to docker
issue_body:
I get no errors, even with debug logging, when using the `MemoryDockerCollector`.

However, I figured out that it was having trouble connecting to docker due to a version mis-match:

```
[root@linux-centos6 diamond]# python -c 'import docker; print docker.Client().containers(all=True)'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/lib/python2.6/site-packages/docker/client.py", line 440, in containers
    res = self._result(self._get(u, params=params), True)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.6/site-packages/docker/client.py", line 126, in _result
    self._raise_for_status(response)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.6/site-packages/docker/client.py", line 122, in _raise_for_status
    raise errors.APIError(e, response, explanation=explanation)
docker.errors.APIError: 404 Client Error: Not Found ("client and server don't have same version (client : 1.18, server: 1.17)")

issue_title: Failure to merge multiple pipelines automatically in query when pipeline parameters are involved
issue_body:
The issue was reported by @jean997 over slack:

Lets say I have two tracks that get run A*B and A*C. Lets say that C has three possible parameter values and B only has 1. Right now if I do something like
```
dscout <- dscquery(dsc.outdir="results",
                   targets=c("A.A_params", "B.summary", "C.C_params", "C.summary"))
```

I will only get results for the first parameter value for C but if I do

```
dscout <- dscquery(dsc.outdir="results",
                   targets=c("A.A_params",  "C.C_params", "C.summary"))
```
I get all three. This makes sense but it is awkward because I have to merge data frames

My response:

This is a result of joining in tables by common key when constructing SQL query. But I suppose I can break it up smarter into multiple SQL queries more properly, and join separate tables, then merge the outcome internally, so that you'll not have to merge tables outside the query function.

issue_title: Request: do not store domains in etcd:/deis/domains
issue_body:
Currently, when a domain is added for an app, the relationship between that app and that domain is stored in _two_ places: the database (makes sense) and etcd under the key `/deis/domains` (might not make quite as much sense).

The `deis-router`, ultimately needs to be aware of these relationships and I believe it runs confd in the background to update routes based on what's found in etcd under the `/deis/domains` key.

The problem with this is that anyone who's seeking to externalize durable platform state using the likes of RDS and S3 has no way of externalizing this little bit of configuration. It has, at least once, left me tearing down a cluster that I presumed to be stateless, only to replace it with another brand new cluster, backed by the same externalized datastores only to find that some of my routes are broken.  Deleting and re-adding the domains fixes the situation, but that's less than ideal.

Can the router source this information from the database instead?

#3486 is strikingly similar in nature, but pertains to the builder.  These two issues probably call for one common solution.

issue_title: Investigate moving off `six`, along with SDK

Per globus/globus-sdk-python#51 , we're trying to get off of the dependency on `six` because OSX makes things difficult.
This is largely matter of supporting the naive developers who don't realize that there's a strong reason to use virtualenv.

At present, we're using

- `six.string_types` (easy to sub with some version dispatch)
- `six.reraise`

The `reraise` functionality is hard to imitate, so we'll need to take another look at where and how that's happening. I don't think we can do the current exception handling logic without a `reraise`, but maybe we don't need to preserve context (I think that's just paranoia about how `click` might behave).

issue_title: Extend color palette so that cluster > 12 are not all black
Create new eth_typing lib
issue_body:
This is a follow up of [this discussion](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/pull/459#discussion_r174853525)

### What is wrong?

We currently have many API that take/return `bytes` [1] where we could really benefit from stricter type checking by introducing [`NewType`](http://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/kinds_of_types.html#newtypes)s. In short a `NewType` lets us create a substitute for a type (e.g. `miles` for `int`) so that an API that takes `miles` as inputs can not be called with any arbitrary `int` instead. These kind of errors already killed NASA missions when `km` and `miles` where [accidentally twisted](http://edition.cnn.com/TECH/space/9909/30/mars.metric.02/)


[1] also applies to other types (e.g. `int`) but is mostly problematic with `bytes` in our case

### How can it be fixed

1. Create NewTypes for types that we currently define to loosely (mostly `bytes` but we should check also our usage of `int`)

2. Put them in a new library `eth_typings` that can be distributed on `pypi` so that it can be shared across different projects

issue_title: Type strings only for `encode_single` and `decode_single`
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

The `encode_single` and `decode_single` functions currently accept either a type string or a type tuple to identify the type that is being worked with.  It seems preferable that a type string is all that should be accepted since type tuples are simple converted to type strings internally and an additional type check is performed on the input which could become costly if it ends up in a critical path.

### How can it be fixed?

We should consider changing `encode_single` and `decode_single` to only accept type strings and also remove the `collapse_type` utility function.  Since this function is extremely simple in nature, it can be manually copied to and used in whatever contexts it is still needed (such as in web3.py code).

issue_title: Should zero-length tuple types be a valid type?
issue_body:
*Note: This issue ticket doesn't follow the usual format because I couldn't think of a way to fit it that.*

In the [solidity ABI spec](https://solidity.readthedocs.io/en/develop/abi-spec.html#types), it appears that zero-length tuple types are a valid type:

> (T1,T2,...,Tn): tuple consisting of the types T1, …, Tn, n >= 0

If that was intentional, then should it be assumed that this is some kind of [unit type](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unit_type) (e.g. like `None` in Python or `()` in Haskell)?

Additionally, if it's correct to see zero-length tuples as unit types, how should we support that?  I suppose an encoder for the unit type could just encode `b'\x00'` in every case and just ignore whatever Python value it is given to encode.  A decoder could also just return `None` by default in every case for the decoded value but still consume and ignore 256 bits of data.

issue_title: Events filtering not working with EIP checksum addresses (Ganache-CLI)
issue_body:
* Version: 4.0.0b10 - 4.1.0
* Python: 3.5
* OS: linux


### What was wrong?

Maybe the issue is not exactly web3.py related, but when using EIP-55 checksum addresses event filtering is not working. My guess is that the reason is that Ganache-CLI is not producing proper addresses.

_Event Filter done as follows:_

```python
certificate_created_filter = instance.eventFilter(
    'CertificateCreated',
    {
        'fromBlock': 0,
        'toBlock': 'latest',
    })
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
try:
    task = loop.create_task(
        self.log_loop(certificate_created_filter, 0.5))
    loop.run_until_complete(task)
finally:
    loop.close()
```
_Checking for events_
```python
    async def log_loop(self, event_filter, poll_interval):
        event_filter.get_all_entries()
        while True:
            for event in event_filter.get_new_entries():
                self.handle_event(event)
            await asyncio.sleep(poll_interval)
```

_Not Working:_

```python
for _, network in contract_interface['networks'].items():
    _contracts[contract_name] = w3.eth.contract(
        address=web3.Web3.toChecksumAddress(network['address']),
        abi=contract_interface['abi'])
    return _contracts[contract_name]
```

_Working (with monkey patched web3.utils.validation.validate_address):_

```python
for _, network in contract_interface['networks'].items():
    _contracts[contract_name] = w3.eth.contract(
        address=network['address'],  # Address all lowercased
        abi=contract_interface['abi'])
    return _contracts[contract_name]
```

The monkey patch is to simply ignore the EIP checksum validation.


### How can it be fixed?

Not sure what is the best way to fix it. Simply ignoring checksum validation seems not right. I'm glad to discuss.

_editor: Let's add an optional middleware that can be injected to the innermost layer that forces all hex to lowercase, similar to the `geth_poa_middleware`_

issue_title: Add caching middleware
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

Please include any of the following that are applicable:

For dapps that aggressively poll for state, adding a caching layer can greatly help to reduce the load that gets put on the node. This is implemented as a [subprovider](https://github.com/MetaMask/provider-engine/blob/master/subproviders/cache.js) in web3.js, and it would be great if something similar existed in web3.py

### How can it be fixed?

web3.py should have an optional middleware that caches rpc requests and gets invalidated on new blocks or reorgs.

but the actual scenario is 

test-all:
	tox

### How can it be fixed?

either change test-all to testall or testall to test-all.

import pytest

from web3 import Web3
from web3.providers.eth_tester import EthereumTesterProvider
from web3.contract import ImplicitContract
import json

w3 = Web3(EthereumTesterProvider())

@pytest.fixture
def a():
    return w3.personal.listAccounts

@pytest.fixture
def contracts():
    with open('../contracts.json', 'r') as f:
        contracts = json.loads(f.read())
    return contracts['contracts']

@pytest.fixture
def Contract(contracts):

    class Contract:
        def __init__(self, contract_name):
            interface = contracts[contract_name]
            self.abi = interface['abi']
            self.bin = interface['bin']
            self.runtime = interface['bin-runtime']
            self.factory = w3.eth.contract(abi=self.abi, bytecode=self.bin)

        def deploy(self, *args, **kwargs):
            tx_hash = self.factory.deploy(args=args, **kwargs)
            tx_receipt = w3.eth.getTransactionReceipt(tx_hash)
            address = tx_receipt['contractAddress']
            return w3.eth.contract(self.abi, address,
                    ContractFactoryClass=ImplicitContract)


    return Contract
```

test_contract.py:
```python
def test_contract(Contract):
    Contract('contracts/ContractA.sol:ContractA').deploy()
```

issue_title: Making conda the ultimate package manager
issue_body:
I have been challenged a number of times on why I don't use conda for everything I do. 

The following may be a bit long winded, but I think that this pretty short given my goal.
### Problem statement

Sometimes if all I need to do is pip install something especially when provisioning new servers, then I won't use conda because it is _superfluous_. 
#### Example

When my install command is just `pip install -r requirements.txt` If I wanted to use conda I would have to:

1) Download and install conda
2) Split out packages in requirements.txt that are in PyPI and not in a conda channel. OR create conda packages for each PyPI package.
3) Add a new command `conda install` 

I could do this, it is not _unreasonable_. But why would I do the extra work, what advantage does this give me? What advantage does conda give users over pip for packages that are easy to install?
### End Goal

_Make conda the ultimate package manager_ Install all packages with `conda`. no one should ever need anything else. All package should be treated as equals.
#### Example

`conda install package1 package2` should install package1 and package2 the same if they are pip or conda (In the future yum, nodejs, etc too)
### One Solution

Revisit the ideas behind `conda pip` (not the command itself) now that `binstar` exists.

I know that it is less than ideal to have to support multiple package types. but If we want to take over the world, we have to start here. Once people are using conda - even tho they may be installing pip packages - we will make it easy for them to upload new **conda** packages. and thus fulfilling our goal of world domination.
#### Binstar background

Binstar is a package hosting tool that supports arbitrary package types and package metadata. binstar packages are name-spaced behind a user account allowing multiple users to upload the same packages if they like.

Binstar packages contain `distribution`s that are of a package type (conda or pypi, etc.) and contains metadata formatted in a binstar common metadata format:

The binstar structure looks like this:

```
+ sean 
        + ipython
              + ipython-0.13.1-py26_1.tar.bz2
                    + binstar metadata for conda package
              + ipython-0.13.1.tar.gz
                    + binstar metadata for pypi package
```

To work natively with package managers, binstar provides filters and translators in the form of sister sites (e.g. pypi.binstar.org and conda.binstar.org) Theses sister sites transform the package metadata into something the package manager expects:

So to install Ipython with `pip` I could do:

`pip install ipython --index-url https://pypi.binstar.org/sean`

OR, to install it with `conda` I could do:

`conda install ipython --channel https://conda.binstar.org/sean`

There is also another sister site `api.binstar.org` which provides the highest degree of control over all of these packages.

**Note:** There is also another level of package access control. The above example assumes that IPython is a publicly accessible package. binstar also has private packages. The procedure to install a private package is:

``` bash
TOKEN=$(binstar auth --create -n 'my conda deployment token' --resource 'conda:**')
conda install ipython --channel https://$TOKEN@conda.binstar.org/sean
```
#### Implementation: Refactoring Conda to take advantage of binstar:

I would propose that conda be divided in to the following 4 composable modules, just like compiler commands are composed of multiple commands. If these commands are _composable_ then we can do a refactor first without changing anything and then modifying the components to suit our needs:
1. Fetch package metadata and package dependency tree
2. Compute actions
3. Fetch packages into cache
4. Apply actions
##### Fetch package metadata and package dependency tree

This one is pretty simple, but for binstar, the dependency tree may be different if a 
user is logged in or not. For binstar, this includes all 'types' of packages like pypi or conda.
##### Compute actions

This module would take the metadata combined with metadata of currently installed packages and output a manifest describing (step by step) exactly what needs to be done on this environment.

For example a manifest may look something like:

```
CONDA: uninstall $CACHE/sean/ipython-0.13.1-py26_1.tar.bz2
CONDA: install   $CACHE/sean/ipython-1.0.1-py26_1.tar.bz2
PYPI:  install   $CACHE/ilan/flask-0.9.0.tar.gz
```

This would specify the actual paths of the files to be installed and would ignore all dependancies. (the execution order matters)
##### Fetch packages into cache

Because some of the packages may be private, the installers should not have to fetch the packages into the cache. this should be done first 
##### Apply actions

Apply each command in the manifest in order. choosing the correct installer and running that command
(Let me know if this is clear)

cc @teoliphant @ilanschnell @ijstokes @joelhullcio 

<!---
@huboard:{"order":0.007568359375,"custom_state":""}
-->

issue_title: System python PATH set to python3
issue_body:
I tried to install multiple packages using conda , after installing it with pip,  when I tried to `$python`
I end up getting python3. I believe this is causing issues to my system. the error is described here

http://askubuntu.com/questions/819476/errors-occurring-when-trying-to-install-python-command-not-found

It would also be nice if somebody could help me set my system python path to 2 again too

issue_title: source activate not safe to be run by multiple shells concurrently.
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...** - [x] bug report - [ ] feature request ## Current Behavior I have the line `source activate my_conda` at the end of my .bash_profile on a particular linux cluster. When I start multiple jobs on the cluster, when they source .bash_profile, more than one can hit that line at the same time. When they do, various kinds of badness can happen. Here is a sample output: ``` /astro/u/mjarvis/.conda/envs/my_conda/bin/deactivate: /astro/u/mjarvis/.conda/envs/my_conda/bin/conda: /gpfs01/astro/packages/anaconda/1.8.0/bin/python: bad interpreter: No such file or directory /astro/u/mjarvis/.conda/envs/my_conda/bin/activate: line 94: sed: command not found /astro/u/mjarvis/.conda/envs/my_conda/bin/activate: line 98: grep: command not found /astro/u/mjarvis/.conda/envs/my_conda/bin/activate: line 99: grep: command not found ``` or this ``` /gpfs01/astro/packages/anaconda/1.8.0/bin/python: can't open file '/astro/u/mjarvis/.conda/envs/my_conda/bin/conda': [Errno 2] No such file or directory ``` or this ``` /gpfs01/astro/packages/anaconda/1.8.0/bin/python: can't open file '/astro/u/mjarvis/.conda/envs/my_c onda/bin/conda': [Errno 2] No such file or directory /astro/u/mjarvis/.conda/envs/my_conda/bin/activate: line 97: [: =: unary operator expected ``` or this ``` /astro/u/mjarvis/.conda/envs/my_conda/bin/deactivate: line 84: /astro/u/mjarvis/.conda/envs/my_conda/bin/conda: No such file or directory /astro/u/mjarvis/.conda/envs/my_conda/bin/activate: line 65: /astro/u/mjarvis/.conda/envs/my_conda/bin/conda: No such file or directory ``` It seems that the problem is that some part of doing `source activate my_conda` involves deleting and recreating the symlinks `conda`, `activate` and `deactivate` in the `.conda/envs/my_conda/bin` directory. So other shells get fubared by these links being changed or deleted while they are trying to use them. ### Steps to Reproduce ``` source activate my_conda ls -lrt .conda/envs/my_conda/bin/ ``` Note the time stamps of the last three items. They are all brand new. ## Expected Behavior These links should be made when first doing `conda create my_conda` and then not modified by `source activate my_conda`. ## Environment Information
`conda info`

``` $ conda info Current conda install: platform : linux-64 conda version : 4.3.30 conda is private : False conda-env version : 4.3.30 conda-build version : not installed python version : 2.7.11.final.0 requests version : 2.14.2 root environment : /gpfs01/astro/packages/anaconda/1.8.0 (read only) default environment : /astro/u/mjarvis/.conda/envs/my_conda envs directories : /astro/u/mjarvis/.conda/envs /gpfs01/astro/packages/anaconda/1.8.0/envs package cache : /gpfs01/astro/packages/anaconda/1.8.0/pkgs /astro/u/mjarvis/.conda/pkgs channel URLs : https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/main/linux-64 https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/main/noarch https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/linux-64 https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/noarch https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/r/linux-64 https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/r/noarch https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/linux-64 https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/noarch config file : None netrc file : /astro/u/mjarvis/.netrc offline mode : False user-agent : conda/4.3.30 requests/2.14.2 CPython/2.7.11 Linux/2.6.32-696.18.7.el6.x86_64 Scientific Linux/6.4 glibc/2.12 UID:GID : 10388:8303 $ ```

issue_title: Noarch for C/C++ (e.g. header only)
issue_body:
A common use case in C/C++ code is to make a header only library. The intent generally being some mixture of being as portable as possible, being easy to use, being easy to depend on, or being as flexible as possible.

In practice they are installed in `$PREFIX/include` or `%LIBRARY_INC%`. Though they may install other things like pkg-config files or CMake files to aid in discovery. Occasionally they may ship with docs as well. As the layout between Windows and Unix packages in `conda`/`conda-build` is not one-to-one, we are unable to use `noarch: generic` for these (even though we might like to).

So having some support from `conda`/`conda-build` to move these files into the right location for the platform being installed on would be very helpful. Alternatively determining a layout that would work for both platforms and ensuring `conda`/`conda-build` would know about this layout and respect it would be very helpful.

issue_title: exit code 0 (success) despite schema error
issue_body:
using curator version 5.5.4, we had rolled out a configuration by accident with the following content:
``` 
  1-logstash-access-misc-1:
    action: rollover
    description: rollover index logstash-access-misc
    options:
      name: logstash-access-misc-write
      conditions:
        max_docs: 10gb
      continue_if_exception: True
      extra_settings:
        index.number_of_shards: 1
```

since this is wrong (max_docs needs to be changed to max_size), curator exits without doing any action:
```
{"@timestamp": "2018-07-31T09:44:26.142Z", "function": "result", "linenum": 65, "loglevel": "ERROR", "message": "Schema error: expected int for dictionary value @ data['conditions']['max_docs']", "name": "curator.validators.SchemaCheck"}
```

unfortunately, curator doesn't exit with exit code 1 as described in the documentation:
https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/5.5/exit-codes.html
because of this, the test (dry-run) done in our deployment pipeline did not block the release.

issue_title: [META] Rollover indices lifecycle
issue_body:
## Rollover indices

By rollover indices we mean a group of indices and an alias that relies on the
`_rollover` API to operate. We can assume that each group has a single alias
`PREFIX` (for example `"prod-apache-logs"`) and a series of indices that follow the naming pattern of
`PREFIX-\d{6}` (`"prod-apache-logs-000001"` - `"prod-apache-logs-000007"`). The 
alias points to the last index in the series, this index
(`"prod-apache-logs-000007"`) is the index that is still being written to, we
call that the `active` index.

### Lifecycle

In various points in an index lifecycle we might want to perform operation on
either `active` or `inactive` indices, for example:

* snapshot all `active` indices every hour
* force merge indices that are no longer `active`

We also need to address indices that belong to a certain time period. Since a
rollover index can belong to multiple time periods (as rollover is often
size-based and not time-based) we need to identifyy possibly all of them to be
able to: 

* snapshot all indices containing January data into `archive-201701` repository


## Proposed enhancements

- [x] add `intersect` value to `stats_result` parameter of the `period` filter
which will identify those indices where `min_value <= range_to AND max_value >=
range_from` (the `period` of the filter and of the index data intersect) (#1045)

- [x] add `pattern` parameter to `count` filter that would allow it to operate
within groups: given indices `[logs-1, logs-2, logs-3, metrics-1, metrics-2]`
the filter `{filtertype: count, count: 1, pattern: '^(.*)-\d$', reverse: true}` will
produce `[logs-3, metrics-2]` - a list composed of `active` indices for
every prefix. (#1044)

 - [x] allow absolute time for `period` filter where we cannot filter for same by simple `pattern` filter like we can for time-based indices.(#1047)

issue_title: [DNS] Change 'az network dns record update-soa' to 'az network dns record soa update'
issue_body:
Under 'az network dns record', we have a bunch of subgroups (one for each record type) followed by a one-off command 'update-soa'.

The SOA record set is a bit of a special case, in that it can be edited but doesn't support add/remove.

However, I think there's a cleaner way to support this. Remove the 'update-soa' command, and move it to an 'update' command under the 'soa' command group

That way, under 'records' we have a clean list of command groups, one for each record type including SOA. Under each record type, we support add/remove commands, except for SOA which supports an update command instead.

issue_title: azure.storage.QueueMessage considered harmful
issue_body:
### Description:
This week I wrote code addressing Azure Storage Blobs, Tables and Queues. Great stuff. Since I ran into errors while removing message that were dequeued more often than my limit, I looked into the details of azure.storage.QueueMessage. The implementation was surprising me...

azure.storage.QueueMessage is just a bunch of strings. I was really surprised that this class (obviously nothing more than a Data Transfer Object) ships with an SDK that is marked as 'production/stable/ on PyPI. 


class QueueMessage(WindowsAzureData):

    ''' Queue message class. '''

    def __init__(self):
        self.message_id = u''
        self.insertion_time = u''
        self.expiration_time = u''
        self.pop_receipt = u''
        self.time_next_visible = u''
        self.dequeue_count = u''
        self.message_text = u''

I would expect members having names containing 'time' to have type DateTime and members having names indicating an integer (dequeue_count) to be an Integer. Instances of this class are just prone to error. That's what happened to me.

Focused Python developers may expect a standard-level pythonic API coming from Microsoft that of course will hide transport implementations details as well that are discussed in this issue [https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/150]. 

Can anybody comment this or explain why it is like that or even has to be like that? Any suggestions how to contribute on that important topic without breaking APIs are welcome.

##### Original Issue: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/156
### Comments (0):

issue_title: Better support for multiple projects with different language versions
issue_body:
Consider the following situation:

System python2.7
Project 1 has repo1 with sha1 and hook1 configured with python3.4
Project 2 has repo1 with sha1 and hook1 configured with default python version (system python2.7)

In ~/.pre-commit/ if project 1's hooks are run first, the repo created will have a python3.4 interpreter.  If project 2 goes to run the hooks, they'll continue to use the interpreter intended for project1, not the interpreter specified in project2.

I think the best way to approach this is to include the interpreter version in the virtualenv directory.  This'll allow (in this example) a `venv-python3.4` and a `venv-default` to live side-by-side and not clobber each other.

issue_title: coala

Hi,

we are currently writing a language independent static code analysis framework named coala - coala-analyzer.org . As part of that goal we offer a unified user interface to lots of common linters and provide stuff like json output and so on for them.

I think pre-commit could profit from coala in that you would not have to maintain that many tool integrations anymore since coala could act as a proxy here (we do that anyways). On long term that is less work for you and for your users because they can configure everything in one config file, don't need to learn multiple tools.

issue_title: Null Space Inverse Kinematics has problems with fixed joints
issue_body:
I believe the issue found and fixed in Inverse Dynamics with fixed joints (https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/issues/1379) may also be present in Null Space Inverse Kinematics.

In particular, `lowerLimits`, `upperLimits`, `jointRanges`, `restPoses` are expected to have lengths equal to the number of joints by `calculateInverseKinematics` : see e.g.  https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/master/examples/pybullet/pybullet.c#L8120

But once passed to bullet, it looks like the number of dofs is expected instead: e.g. https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/abeae7e1e7e23d5ba8e840e98b46470d4162a73b/examples/SharedMemory/PhysicsClientC_API.cpp#L4001

Unfortunately padding the arrays until they are length numJoints (as suggested in the previous issue) doesn't seem to fix this one.

I am still finding my way around the code base so apologies if I've read this wrong. Thanks again for your time and help!

issue_title: Server keep_alive is too aggressive
issue_body:
## Long story short

We run an aiohttp server behind an ALB, the ALB maintains a keep-alive connection between the ALB and the backend, the current implementation of the server side keep-alive timeout in aiohttp triggers unexpected connection closes that propagate out to the clients.

## Expected behaviour

The server should allow the client (the ALB in this case) a chance to close the connection by responding with the appropriate `keep-alive:close` header i.e. after the timeout hits I would expect that instead of calling `self.force_close` the force_close flag gets set so that the next response can respond with the close header - triggering the force close on the second run of the timer seems reasonable, but as designed on a  busy service behind a load balancer this is implementation will just randomly terminate valid connections every 75 seconds if they're unlucky enough.

## Actual behaviour

The `RequestHandler` registers a timer that runs every 75 seconds, if it see no active use of the connection it disconnects the underlying connection, but this exposes a race condition where the client has already started making a request and will now recieve a `aiohttp.client_exceptions.ClientConnectorError`

## Steps to reproduce

Run a trivial aiohttp service, for quicker repro modify the default value of `keepalive_timeout` on the RequestHandler to one second, then run a simple script that tries to re-use a connection making requests that are likely to coincide with the timeout value:

```
async def test_client():
	session = aiohttp.ClientSession()
	while True:
		async with session.get('https://localhost.fbn.org/foo') as r:
			result = await r.text()
			print(result)
		await asyncio.sleep(random.uniform(0.99, 1.01))
```

## Your environment

aiohttp 2.1.0, but the implementation of `process_keepalive` in 2.3.9 still calls `force_close`

issue_title: Raise an exception if trying to write into closed response
issue_body:
See https://stackoverflow.com/questions/47175297/handling-premature-client-disconnection-in-aiohttp

The problem is: `await drain()` does nothing because the buffer is not overflown, the buffer is not overflown because sent data are ignored and not accumulated in internal buffer.
`transport.is_closing()` should be used for check.

It should be fixed in payload writer, affects both client and server.

issue_title: Setting response body using middleware leads to inconsistent Content-Length
issue_body:
## Long story short

I am using simple middleware handler that changes response body. `'CONTENT-LENGTH'` header is being set before response hits the middleware. There is a code in body setter that prevents this header from being changed if it was already set:

```python
     @body.setter
    def body(self, body,
             CONTENT_TYPE=hdrs.CONTENT_TYPE,
             CONTENT_LENGTH=hdrs.CONTENT_LENGTH):
            ...
            # set content-length header if needed
            if not self._chunked and CONTENT_LENGTH not in headers:
                size = body.size
                if size is not None:
                    headers[CONTENT_LENGTH] = str(size)
            ...
```

So, after a new body being applied to response, headers still have an old value. This causes issues on a client side - response body being cut, for example.

It is possible to workaround this issue by adding `response.headers.pop(hdrs.CONTENT_LENGTH)` before setting a new body.

## Expected behaviour

Being able to set body during a middleware handling properly.

## Actual behaviour

`'CONTENT-LENGTH'` header not being updated - causes issues on a client side.

## Steps to reproduce

Using aiohttp.test_utils. This example fails with `json.decoder.JSONDecodeError` due to reponse.content being `'{"my_new": "bo'`

```python
import http

from aiohttp import web, hdrs
from aiohttp.test_utils import AioHTTPTestCase, unittest_run_loop
from aiohttp.web_app import Application
from aiohttp.web_middlewares import middleware
from aiohttp.web_request import Request

from aio_service.web.view import BaseView


def body_setter_middleware():

    @middleware
    async def middleware_handler(request: Request, handler: BaseView):
        response = await handler(request)
        # response.headers.pop(hdrs.CONTENT_LENGTH)
        response.body = {'my_new': 'body'}
        return response

    return middleware_handler


class HandlerOK(BaseView):

    async def get(self):
        return web.Response(body={'test': 'ok'})


class TestBodyChanging(AioHTTPTestCase):

    def make_application(self):
        app = Application(loop=self.loop, debug=True)
        app.middlewares.append(body_setter_middleware())

        app.router.add_route('get', '/api/1/ok', HandlerOK)
        return app

    async def get_application(self):
        return self.make_application()

    @unittest_run_loop
    async def test_body_changes(self):
        response = await self.client.request('GET', '/api/1/ok')
        self.assertEqual(response.status, http.HTTPStatus.OK)
        result = await response.json()
        self.assertDictEqual(result, {'my_new': 'body'})

```

## Your environment

aiohttp==3.2.1
MacOS High Sierra 10.13.1

issue_title: WebSocketResponse's timeout does not apply to send_bytes/send_json/send_str calls.
issue_body:
## Long story short

This is more of a question. Is this behaviour by design?

We have to timeout-related parameters on WebSocketResponse:

* `timeout`
* `receive_timeout`

The `receive_timeout` works for all receive operations. Consequently, I assumed that the `timeout` parameter worked for all send operations. Apparently, I was mistaken. The documentation is not so clear about this.

From the look of the implementation, `timeout` is actually only used in the `close()` call.

## Expected/Actual behaviour

I expected that the `timeout` parameter applied to the `send_bytes`/`send_json`/`send_str` calls. It does not.

## Discussion

Currently, I simply wrap my `send_x` calls in `with Timeout(...)`. It's not that much effort but it would be nice to see this directly implemented in aiohttp.

I can see two options:

* Let `timeout` parameter apply to the `send_x` calls
* Add a `send_timeout` parameter to the WebSocketResponse that applied a timeout to the `send_x` calls

This also relates to #2309. In my actual implementation, the `send_x` calls are submitted to the event loop in a fire-and-forget fashion. Consequently, if the connection is abruptly cut, said `send_x` calls are left lingering and never cancelled. By using a timeout, said calls will time out and not fill up in memory.

## Your environment

Aiohttp 2.2.3 and Python 3.6.1.

with (yield from engine) as conn:

issue_title: Example code: Foolbox model predicts different class than underlying Keras model
issue_body:
In the example code provided on GitHub the Foolbox model predicts class 282 for the example image using Keras and ResNet50. 
If you let the underlying Keras model predict the class with

np.argmax(kmodel.predict(image[None,:])

it returns 287. I think this is beacause of the preprocessing done in the Foolbox model. Without said preprocessing both models return class 287. 
Why is this example image used if ResNet50 seems to be unable to classify it correctly without applying some seemingly arbitrary preprocessing?

It seems like the best thing here might be to use another project's url regex. Django, for example, has one that's well-tested and does work on the above url: https://github.com/django/django/blob/f8cc464452f495fce2a3d6f7494396c8f798a1e6/django/core/validators.py#L67

issue_body:
As of master:
```
In [1]: from functools import partial; from autograd import grad

In [2]: def f(x, y): return x

In [3]: grad(partial(f, y=42))
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-3-937070854014> in <module>()
----> 1 grad(partial(f, y=42))

/home/antony/src/extern/autograd/autograd/convenience_wrappers.py in grad(fun, argnum)
     19         return as_scalar(fun(*args, **kwargs))
     20 
---> 21     @attach_name_and_doc(fun, argnum, 'Gradient')
     22     def gradfun(*args,**kwargs):
     23         args = list(args)

/home/antony/src/extern/autograd/autograd/convenience_wrappers.py in attach_name_and_doc(fun, argnum, opname)
    180 def attach_name_and_doc(fun, argnum, opname):
    181     namestr = "{op}_{fun}_wrt_argnum_{argnum}".format(
--> 182         op=opname.lower(), fun=fun.__name__, argnum=argnum)

issue_title: hg should not read from stderr when capturing output
issue_body:
In #104, @kergoth reported that sometimes he sees hg modules grab the wrong rev from reup:

> It seemed related to use of the particular hg modules I'm using, coupled with reup, and possibly also bound to use of the virtualenv I was using for peru. For some reason, hg was complaining about 'obsolete' markers, and peru would pick up 'obsolete' as the new rev.

I'd bet a shiny nickel that the problem is the hg plugin reading from stderr, when it should only be reading from stdout. The `capture_output=True` flag in the internals of that plugin combines the two streams, when it should probably be allowing stderr to print normally. (I think the current behavior is from before we did anything meaningful with plugin output.) Probably any condition where hg prints some random error before its usual output will hit this bug.

issue_title: RFC: Add "publish_github_prerelease" add-ons
issue_body:
### Overview

This add-on allows to automatically publish pre-release on GitHub. 

A pre-release is identified by a git tag and is associated with one or multiple packages (python wheels, installers, archives, ...)

Project maintainers are responsible to graduate a "pre-release" into "release".


###  Use cases

#### Automatic upload of python wheels associated with  [ITKPythonPackage](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage) project

##### Steps

Every day a *bot* will:
* update ITK description and package version in  `ITKPythonPackage` (see details below)
* commit the changes to `ITKPythonPackage`. 

Then, each CI services (or dedicated build bots) will:
* checkout `ITKPythonPackage` source
* build the associated wheels
* use `publish_github_prerelease.py` to publish the generated wheels as pre-release

##### `update_python_version.py` script

This script is not (yet) part of `scikit-ci-addons` and will live in the `ITKPythonPackage` project repository and can be executed as a standalone script. For example, it could then be downloaded and executed by the *bot* named  [kwrobot](https://github.com/kwrobot).

**Usage**

```
Usage:
  python update_python_version.py /path/to/last/ITK/source
```

**Steps**

The *bot* will:
* clone the latest `ITK` source, checkout `nightly-master` branch
* clone `ITKPythonPackage`
* download the script
* execute the script
* commit and push the change to `ITKPythonPackage`

**Script description: update_python_version.py**

Given the patch to both an `ITK` source tree and an `ITKPythonPackage` source tree, the script will:
* get the local commit date of the `HEAD` of [ITK@nightly-master](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/tree/nightly-master). (Using a command like `git show -s --format="%cd" --date=local`)
* get `<major>.<minor>.<patch>` (By extracting the value associated with variables `ITK_VERSION_MAJOR`, `ITK_VERSION_MINOR` and `ITK_VERSION_PATCH` using script like [CMakeParser.py](https://github.com/Slicer/Slicer/blob/eefeac9286f48552e8418a11f412b2823a09b407/Utilities/Scripts/SlicerWizard/CMakeParser.py))
* update [CMakeLists.txt](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage/blob/master/CMakeLists.txt) replacing the ITK version associated with `GIT_TAG` CMake variable to match the latest commit associated with [ITK@nightly-master](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/tree/nightly-master)
* update [setup.py](https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage/blob/master/setup.py) by replacing version<sup>1</sup> `<major>.<minor>.<patch>.dev<YYYYMMDD>` where `<major>.<minor>.<patch>` corresponds the ITK release version and `<YYYYMMDD>` corresponds to the date of the `ITK@nightly-master` commit

<sup>1</sup> To facilitate the update of the version, it could be added to a separate file like `itkVersion.py` or `itk/version.py`

**example of execution**

On unix:

```
# TODO: Delete old virtual env and create a new one (e.g name itk-nightly-wheel)

pip install scikit-ci-addons
git clone git://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage
cd ITKPythonPackage

platform=$(ci_addons publish_github_release --current-platform) # manylinux1, macosx or win
commit_date=$(ci_addons git --commit_date)

./scripts/dockcross-manylinux-build-wheels.sh && \
ci_addons publish_github_release  \
  --clear-pattern "*$platform*.whl" \
  --keep-pattern "*dev$commit_date*.whl" \
  InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage \
  "dist/*dev$commit_date*$platform*.whl"
```

**Remarks:** Invocation of `ci_addons publish_github_release` should probably be moved into  `./scripts/dockcross-manylinux-build-wheels.sh` and `./scripts/macosx-build-wheels.sh`

On windows:

```
$PYTHON_DIR="C:/Python27-x64"
$env:PATH="$PYTHON_DIR;$PYTHON_DIR/Scripts;$env:PATH"

# TODO: Delete old virtual env and create a new one (e.g name itk-nightly-wheel)

pip install scikit-ci-addons
pip install ninja
git clone git://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage
cd ITKPythonPackage

$platform=(ci_addons publish_github_release --current-platform) # manylinux1, macosx or win
$commit_date=(ci_addons git --commit_date)

# Adapted from http://stackoverflow.com/questions/2251622/conditional-execution-and-in-powershell#7272878
$(python setup.py bdist_wheel | Out-Host;$?) -and `
$(
  ci_addons publish_github_release  `
    --clear-pattern "*$platform*.whl" `
    --keep-pattern "*dev$commit_date*.whl" `
    InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITKPythonPackage `
    "dist/*dev$commit_date*$platform*.whl"
| Out-Host;$?)
```

**Remarks:** Invocation of `ci_addons publish_github_release` should probably be moved into  `./scripts/windows-build-wheels.ps1`

issue_title: Feature request: Option to automatically set the user's display name to an LDAP attribute value on first LDAP login
issue_body:
Many LDAP databases contain display names or full names for users. For example:

```
dn: uid=jdoe,ou=users,dc=example,dc=com
objectClass: top
objectClass: inetOrgPerson
objectClass: posixAccount
uidNumber: 1000
gidNumber: 1000
[...]
uid: jdoe
cn: John Doe
```

On first login, a Matrix user is created for the corresponding LDAP account. However, display names are not set by default, so users must enter their own display name. The user in the above example will display as `@jdoe:example.com` until they manually enter a different display name into their client app.

It would be nice if `matrix-synapse-ldap3` could be configured to automatically set users' display names based on their LDAP information on first login. For example, if a server could be configured to set default display names from the `cn` LDAP attribute, the user in the above example would have their default display name set to `John Doe`.

issue_title: Calls to classify end point seem to use 1 API call per string not per list of strings
issue_body:
If I understand the "classify" API documentation correctly, I should be able to pass multiple text strings in a single call and only use 1 API call, correct? I'm basing this on


> This endpoint allows you to perform the classification of many text samples using only one request to a custom or public module.

Assuming my understanding is accurate, I am seeing different behavior. Instead of a single API call for a list of text strings, I see one API call per text string.

This is my sample code

```
from monkeylearn import MonkeyLearn
import user_settings

MONKEYLEARN_API_KEY = user_settings.KEY
MODULE_ID = user_settings.MODULE_ID

ml = MonkeyLearn(MONKEYLEARN_API_KEY)
res = ml.classifiers.detail(MODULE_ID)
samples = ['this is a string',
           'a dog barks on tuesday',
           'i like purple muffins',
           'apples are funny fruit',
           'the house is up a mountain',
           'cats do not like trees',
           'computers will take over',
           'you sank my battleship',
           'duck, duck, goose',
           'yellow baby buggy bumpers']

res = ml.classifiers.classify(MODULE_ID, samples, sandbox=True, debug=False)
print("Quota Remaining")
print("-"*20)
pprint.pprint(res.query_limit_remaining, indent=4)
```

Two runs, back to back, of this print the following:

> RUN 1:
> Quota Remaining
> \--------------------
> '99940'

---
> RUN 2:
> Quota Remaining
> \--------------------
> '99930'

Notice that the remaining quota is 10 apart between runs. This is the same number of sample text strings. If I comment out 5 of them and run it again, I get this output:

> Quota Remaining
> \--------------------
> '99915'

To me, this is confirming that I am using 1 API call to the classify endpoint per string, not per group of strings. 

Is this the expected behavior, or should my list of 10 strings only be costing 1 API call?

issue_title: Change --max-slave-restart option to a sensible value
issue_body:
The default value allows for infinite restarts, but this is a serious problem for suites which are during collection because the test run never finishes.

We should change the default to a sensible number, [@RonnyPfannschmidt suggested](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-xdist/issues/131#issuecomment-323336651) 4 times the number of nodes as default. A value of `0` would mean the previous behavior.

issue_title: Issue with license of Unidecode dependency
issue_body:
A dep conflict we were having with Unidecode led me to a [Wagtail issue](https://github.com/wagtail/wagtail/issues/3311) pointing out that Unidecode is GPLv2+ licensed, a viral license, which is incompatible with Oscar's BSD license.

We're using Unidecode to sanely strip unicode chars in our custom [slugify](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/core/utils.py#L38) function.

Without just dropping the `OSCAR_SLUG_ALLOW_UNICODE` setting and allowing unicode slugs everywhere, the only alternative I can see would be to adopt a solution like Wagtail's `cautious_slugifier` ([wagtail/core/utils.py#L64](https://github.com/wagtail/wagtail/blob/f0eef2fc88043ba627b89125876f1cfe53b7fc51/wagtail/core/utils.py#L64))?

issue_title: username attribute in UserFactory model
issue_body:
As far as I can tell, the `username` attribute in the `UserFactory` model should be removed:

https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/test/factories/customer.py#L19

The `User` model in Oscar does not include a field called `username` - and even includes a comment saying `username` has been removed:

https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/apps/customer/abstract_models.py#L49

When using the `UserFactory` in our tests, it causes an exception in factory_boy when trying to instantiate a `User` model. I had to copy the factory into our project and remove the `username` attribute to get it working.

issue_title: Rename the auth command
issue_body: Rename the auth command to something more meaningful. Auth might make users expect to have to create an account or to login to a service provider. Make it certonly.

issue_title: Don't add extra ServerName/ServerAlias directives
issue_body:
With #2537, we correctly detect wildcard `ServerAlias` directives when choosing a vhost, however, we still add a specific `ServerName/ServerAlias` even though it's not necessary.

For example if we have:
```
ServerAlias *.example.com
```

After we install the certifiicate we'll have:
```
ServerAlias *.example.com
ServerAlias www.example.com
```

We shouldn't do this as the change is unnecessary and (slightly) complicates the resulting vhost.

issue_title: Create base resource
issue_body:
This ticket is to create the base resource - a class that can be used directly or subclassed to create wrappers with some syntactic sugar.

* User can invoke the base resource to call any API available and pass through any parameters available
* User can invoke any of the supported methods on a given API (SP supports only GET, POST, PUT, DELETE)
* Custom exception class is thrown when an error in the API occurs
* Responses must pass through to user without modification for both success and failure

issue_title: bash() in replwrap.py expects bash prompt to end with $ or #?
issue_body:
I was playing around with a Jupyter Notebook with the metakernel_bash kernel, and I noticed that the kernel took an unusually long time to load, and the notebook wouldn't work as expected (i.e., not like a bash prompt). In the log, I was getting an error message having to do with shell_magic.py. 

After some digging around, it seems that bash() in replwrap.py expects my bash prompt to end with $ or #. I had a custom prompt that didn't end in either of those characters. Changing my custom prompt to end with $ solved the problem.

I'm not sure what's the best way to solve this - just thought you should know.

issue_title: Final base of transcript reference sequences is not handled correctly on mapping
issue_body:
HI,

I have noticed that the final base of transcript (possibly other) reference sequences is mishandled when mapping c_to_g.

Considering transcript NM_000088.3
hgvs will correctly normalize  NM_000088.3:c.*1400_*1406= to NM_000088.3:c.*1400_*1406AACATAA=, and will also convert to an n. position e.g. NM_000088.3:n.5921_5927AACATAA=. However, mapping fails when trying to map to the genome. "HGVSInvalidIntervalError: start or end or both are beyond the bounds of transcript record."

Slightly different, but possibly related, Considering transcript NM_001145051.2
NM_001145051.2:c.659dupA should normalize to NM_001145051.2:c.660dupA. However NM_001145051.2:c.660A= is considered out of bounds. Although the metadata for this transcript implies that the final A base of the transcript has been added to complete the stop codon, the reference sequence (corroborated by SeqRepo) contains the A base at c.660 so the error "HGVSInvalidIntervalError: start or end or both are beyond the bounds of transcript record" is incorrect. Is this particular a conscious decision based on the metadata, or is it a bug?

Thanks

Pete

issue_title: Better Asyncio Support
issue_body:
I noticed there's an `aio.py` module in this project that uses the nonblocking mode of the serial connection in order to support use with asyncio. I tried using this on my Mac and my desktop (running Ubuntu 14.04) and got mixed results. For some reason, on my Mac I could only read in single garbage character `b'0xfe' or `b'0xff` and I didn't seem to write correctly. That may or may not be an issue with the `aio.py` code but might have been some other problem.

Either way, I didn't like that the nonblocking mode doesn't support windows so I wrote my own asyncio adapter layer over pyserial using a separate worker thread to handle the blocking serial calls. You can see the code in this gist: https://gist.github.com/dummerbd/75316ab8c109b5bbc46b

If my code looks like a good addition for this library, I can clean it up a bit (like add some logging statements) and make a pull request. If not, let me know what you don't like!

issue_title: Explicit Feedback Recommender
issue_body:
Love this LightFM library - thank you!

I have used it to build a hybrid content-based and collaborative filtering model. My interactions matrix contains both positive (+1) and negative (-1) interactions as our customers were requested to vote yes or no on the items. Items that they have not interacted with yet should not be classed as a negative (as this is an Explicit Matrix Factorisation problem).

Am I doing something wrong here or is my understanding incorrect and it cannot be used for Explicit problems?

Thanks in advance for any advice.

issue_title: Version identifier for development versions
issue_body:
We use Z3 in a version that is always compiled from a recent git revision instead of a stable release. It would be convenient for us if a Z3 (library) binary could provide more precise information about its version, such that we can for example check whether we actually used the desired version from our log afterwards. Currently, Z3 reports the version "4.4.2.1", which does not point to a specific commit.

Many projects use a version string that is based on `git describe`, which is very convenient (currently this would be `z3-4.4.1-1594-g56c7875`). It contains the name of the latest stable release, it is globally unique because of the git hash, and strictly monotone in many use cases (as long as always building from master), because the `1594` is the number of commits since the `z3-4.4.1` tag. It would be great if Z3 could report this or a similar string.

I found the `--githash` parameter for the build system, which seems to accept not only hashes, but arbitrary strings, so I could simply always build Z3 with `--githash=$(git describe)`. Unfortunately, however, I did not find a way to retrieve this string via the API. It seems to be reported only when calling `z3 -h`. So one solution would be to add an API function for retrieving this string.

unsat
(as22val as44val namedP1t namedP2t namedP3t)

issue_title: What version of Theano should Lasagne require/support?
issue_body:
I think Lasagne's `requirements.txt` should point to a git commit, updated with each PR to the latest version. This provides a fairly current Theano, while still making sure we don't pull in changes which might break Lasagne.

Arguments for this:
* 0.7 is several months old, with > 1000 commits since. 
* The last release gap was over 1 year, and I see no reason to expect that to change.
* While Theano's docs ostensibly refer to 0.7, they're actually compiled from master, and contain information on code that doesn't exist in 0.7. I think this is evidence that git is the definitive version.

issue_title: squared_error: Warn if comparing column vector against row vector
issue_body:
At least one user got trapped by setting target_var to a vector for a regression task, and then passing it to lasagne.objectives.squared_error along with the network predictions for a network of a single output unit: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/lasagne-users/DsSY1cHCC2M

Due to #715, Theano knows that the network predictions are a column vector (a matrix with broadcast pattern (False, True)), so it will happily broadcast the network predictions and the targets into a square matrix of (batchsize, batchsize) and compute the squared differences between every prediction and every target (as opposed to computing the squared differences between corresponding predictions and targets only). The mean of the loss is still a scalar, so training will appear to work, but not learn anything.

We should catch this case and warn the user to use a column vector for the squared error targets.

issue_title: workflow to add `Subject` deviates from pattern
issue_body:
There seem to be two alternative workflows to add data to an `NWBFile` object. The first is to construct the file with the minimum required arguments:
```python
from pynwb import NWBFile
from datetime import datetime
NWBFile(source='source', session_description='session_description',
        identifier='identifier', session_start_time=datetime.now())
```
and then add data as you build it. This is the strategy demonstrated in the tutorials. The second method is to build up the data first and then construct the `NWBFile` object later with the data as arguments. As far as I can tell, all of the data types can be put into the `NWBFile` using either method, except `Subject`, which must be added the second way: constructed beforehand and added during construction of `NWBFile`, because there is no `add_subject` or `create_subject` method in `NWBFile`. I think it's fine that we have two options, and I don't really have a preference for either workflow. You can currently add Subject using the second workflow no problem, even if the rest of the data uses the first, but if I think if the tutorials only teach the first then we should make all of the datatypes compatible with that workflow. Was this an intentional design decision?
