issue_title: Satpy/pyresample use cases
issue_body:
I believe I could reuse pyresample, satpy or other packages in pytroll, but I need some guidance.

I am working on generating satellite albedo products and I need to process many (all?) available satellite data, Level 2 and Level 3 (full archives), reproject them on a common grid, compare, etc.

More precisely, we want to read, preprocess, reproject to a common grid the METOP-AVHRR data, MSG-SEVIRI, MODIS collection 5 and 6, and SPOT-VGT albedo datasets; the grids being either

- plate carree
- METOP grid
- MSG geostationary grid.

Then, we want to stack the harmonised data through time and store these datacubes efficiently to be able to read them rather quickly when we need to compare/plot results. We plan to use the xarray/dask?/pandas/seaborn/bokeh/datashader?/holoviews ecosystem.

In order to have a generic tool allowing us to project from whatever grid to any other grid, we have started to use pyresample. 

1) So far, I’ve been struggling with grid and projection definitions for sinusoidal and geostationary products like those from METOP & MSG. **Are these grids already defined somewhere in the satpy code ?**

2) Also we plan to suffer on the optimisation of data reading and storage optimisation for such big data files as the METOP (18001 x 36000 pixels, full globe 1km) & MODIS global datasets. **How far are you in using dask to improve this ?**

3) We also find out about the satpy package you’re developing, and which seems to offer readers for most meteo satellites available. I do not have yet a very clear views of the possibilities offered by Satpy and was wondering if you think that the tools you propose could be directly used for our objectives or if it is preferable for us to either use some specific functions of the package (like pyresample, and probably others), and maybe borrow some other code here and there, to develop our own tool. If so, perhaps could you point me to the satpy functions that could be of interest for us. Basically, **is it a good use case fo satpy : should I reuse satpy as a user or go in the code to steal the logic ?**

Would you rather chat on slack or should I ask question on stackoverflow or open a github issue or call by phone ?

Please do not hesitate to tell me that you are busy and don't have time for this.

issue_title: setup.py incorrectly detects OpenMP compilation when used with single-threaded OpenBLAS
issue_body:
OpenBLAS compiled in single-threaded mode always contains a routine `openblas_get_num_threads()`, (always returning 1 for single-threaded compilation). This means that the check for a multithreaded libcpptraj on [line 163 of `setup.py`](https://github.com/Amber-MD/pytraj/blob/master/setup.py#L163) incorrectly determines that libcpptraj is compiled with OpenMP support when libcpptraj is linked against OpenBLAS, even when serial compilation of cpptraj is requested (and performed). This currently prevents out-of-the-box Amber16 compilation when linked against OpenBLAS version 0.2.18 on Linux.

A possible fix is to change [line 163 of `setup.py`](https://github.com/Amber-MD/pytraj/blob/master/setup.py#L163) to read
```
libcpptraj_has_openmp = ([line for line in output_openmp_check 
                          if ('get_num_threads' in line.lower() 
                              and not 'openblas_get_num_threads' in line.lower()
                             )]  != [])
```

issue_title: Long waits in Semaphore.aquire when multithreading

I've confirmed that I'm not hitting any app or method API ratelimits, by inspecting the headers of the most recent requests made. 

A better idea of this problem can be seen by viewing this [flame graph](https://drive.google.com/uc?id=1uTjTyNZo5y7MlDEu8B0z0YthMTcnXLi3). Thread 1,2 and 3 are the workers, and they're spending about 70% of their execution time waiting in 'Semaphore.acquire()`. Thread 4-6 are artifacts from profiling.

My issue seems similar to #183, but I'm not sure if it has the same cause. I did some logging of the calls to the Semaphore class, although I don't really understand how it's working, I did notice that `release()` does not get called very often at all.

Is there any multithreading specific setup I could be missing? I couldn't find much documentation about multithreaded setups.

issue_title: Improve parsing for dataframe generation and gracefully handle errors
issue_body:
1. Improve dataframe parsing. In particular, the pyspark livy client should not be calling "eval'.  This may require further investigation into the structure of the strings that Livy may possibly return to the client.

2. Tighten up error handling. This requires enumerating all the possible errors that we may run into during parsing, and converting them smartly into `DataFrameParseExceptions` so that error messages can be displayed to the user nicely.

**Transforming pixel from a depth image to world coordinates**

Hi,
As part of my research, I am using the D415 Realsense camera to capture depth images of an object randomly placed on a table (I am using only depth images). I used image detection to find the exact pixel in the depth image representing the center of the object, and I would like to know how to transform from that pixel position (row, column) in the depth image to the real Cartesian position (x,y) in the world, where the real depth and z are known (the images are always being taken from a certain known position above the table).
I used this code to retrieve to 3D point with respect to 'the center of the physical imager' (as explained [here](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/wiki/Projection-in-RealSense-SDK-2.0#point-coordinates), I tested it with ppx and ppy values):
>pipeline = rs.pipeline()  
config = rs.config()
config.disable_stream(rs.stream.color)
pipeline.start(config)
frames = pipeline.wait_for_frames()
depth_frame = frames.get_depth_frame()
depth_intrin = depth_frame.profile.as_video_stream_profile().intrinsics
depth_value = 0.5
depth_pixel = [depth_intrin.ppx, depth_intrin.ppy]
depth_point = rs.rs2_deproject_pixel_to_point(depth_intrin, depth_pixel, depth_value)
print depth_point
>

I know where I placed the camera above the table, assuming that the center point would be the center of the front side of the camera (where the glass is), and this assumption is wrong.

issue_title: Python 2 support
issue_body:
Would you accept a Python 2 support PR?

It looks like not a lot of code changes are required.

I played around a little, here are my findings:

* the Cython code just compiles and seems to work on Python 2 unchanged

* the ``__all__`` in the ``__init__.py`` fails, because I think ``__all__`` in Cython is a unicode object, something Python 2 doesn't like. I'd advocate changing the ``__init__.py`` to explicitly import everything needed instead of doing a ``from .. import *``. This way you are in full control of what's exposed in the public API.

* the test suite relies on some features from Python 3:

  * in particular ``unitest.mock``, which is available as ``mock`` on PyPI so that's easy
  
  *  ``assertRaisesRegex`` which is missing. It'd need to be backported, or the test rewritten
      as the regexes used aren't complicated.
 
  *  ``subTest``, either backported or the test rewritten.

  I'm a fan of pytest myself so alternatively we can port the tests over over to that; it's compatible
  with Python 2 and Python 3. I'd be happier doing that instead.

* I recommend a tool like tox so we can easily run the tests on both Pythons.

* the Makefile is geared towards Python 3, but doesn't do all that much. I just called the build_ext command myself with Python 2. We could cruftify the makefile to support Python 2. Alternatively we could also just simply document the few standard Python commands needed; tox should care of building stuff and running stuff anyway and that's a simple command.

issue_title: ConnectionField loads the entire query set when paginating
issue_body:
I've noticed that when I query Relay-based pagination it doesn't limit the database query, but loads everything into memory and then does slicing. I understand that for a database table of moderate size it might not be a problem, but it becomes a concern when you've got a few hundred thousands records and want to display only a hundred of them at a time.
Is it something that was missed or this behavior was deliberately omitted for some reason?

Also, so far I got this workaround:

	class Query(ObjectType):
		all_users = relay.ConnectionField(UserConnection)

		def resolve_all_users(self, info, **args):
			_len = User.objects.count()
			return connection_from_list_slice(User.objects.all(), args, 
				connection_type=UserConnection,
				edge_type=UserConnection.Edge, pageinfo_type=relay.PageInfo,
				list_length=_len, list_slice_length=_len)

It is possible to hide this inside a class derived from `ConnectionField` and override `resolve_connection()` which would call `connection_from_list_slice()` instead of `connection_from_list()`. However, this workaround still relies on the current implementation of `connection_from_list_slice()`, which _fortunately_ doesn't do anything that evaluates the passed `QuerySet` until it actually slices it (and only if you provide `list_slice_length` argument to it). From the description of the function it's unsure whether it was specifically designed not to evaluate `QuerySet` or it's just a coincidence. Another downside to it is a separate query that counts rows, but I guess it is a trade off for not fetching everything at once.

So, my question really is: is it a useful feature and if it is, could it be done cleaner (in particular, not relying on `connection_from_list_slice()` implementation)?

issue_body:

However, my stderr is still full of errors when I run my tests (that all pass). I did some digging with the debugger and found this line: https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/f8a6a8a27119f924c2fce863cca87c4219d8a12b/graphql/execution/base.py#L85

Why do we want to print everything to stderr?

**manage.py test output**

```
python manage.py test --settings=config.test_settings
Creating test database for alias 'default'...
System check identified no issues (0 silenced).
..Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/elnygren/Work/sumpli/sumpli-core/ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/graphql/execution/executor.py", line 215, in complete_value_catching_error
    exe_context, return_type, field_asts, info, result)
  File "/Users/elnygren/Work/sumpli/sumpli-core/ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/graphql/execution/executor.py", line 268, in complete_value

graphql.error.located_error.GraphQLLocatedError: No Business matches the given query.
.Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/elnygren/Work/sumpli/sumpli-core/ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/graphql/execution/executor.py", line 215, in complete_value_catching_error
    exe_context, return_type, field_asts, info, result)
  File "/Users/elnygren/Work/sumpli/sumpli-core/ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/graphql/execution/executor.py", line 268, in complete_value
    raise GraphQLLocatedError(field_asts, original_error=result)
graphql.error.located_error.GraphQLLocatedError: 'foo-bar-xyz-abc' is not a valid UUID.
.....................................
```

Note that: 

> No Business matches the given query.  

and

> 'foo-bar-xyz-abc' is not a valid UUID.

are the messages shown to the users.

**Code**

```python
class LikeABusiness(graphene.Mutation):
    """Mutation for liking/following a Business."""
    class Arguments:
        """Arguments of the Mutation"""
        uuid = graphene.String(required=True)

    # output
    ok = graphene.Boolean()

    def mutate(self, info, uuid, *args, **kwargs):
        """Perform the data mutation."""
        try:
            # raises the "No Business matches the given query." message due to 404
            return LikeABusiness(
                ok=get_object_or_404(Business, uuid=uuid).like(info.context.user)
            )
        except ValidationError as e:
            # passes along the "'foo-bar-xyz-abc' is not a valid UUID." message
            raise Exception(e.messages[0])

issue_title: SerializerMutation raises `GraphQLLocatedError` on `cls.perform_mutate`
Docs instruct you to install *wrong* version leading to unnecessary confusion & broken code
issue_body:
http://docs.graphene-python.org/projects/django/en/latest/tutorial-plain/#setup-the-django-project

**Actual:**
> `pip install graphene_django`

**Shoulde be (like in the README):**
> `pip install "graphene-django>=2.0.dev"`

**Causes:**

Reader will install graphene_django 1.3 while the docs are about 2.0. Things like Mutations will break in non-obvious ways.

**FIX:**

The docs should (for now) tell people to use the `>=2.0.dev` postfix.

issue_title: [ncp] Add a public method to ncp_base that allows vendor code to send Spinel messages to a host.
issue_body:
In order to allow vendor specific code to send Spinel packets to a host asynchronously, a new method must be added to the ncp_base class.  This method will be public and callable from vendor code. Like VendorCommandHandler this new method will be implemented by the vendor to perform vendor requirements with full access to the ncp public and private methods and members. 

This will allow vendor code to send spinel packets up to the host without having to implement new code to Openthread.

The new method will have to take a void* context as an input parameter which will represent the content that is understood by the vendor code calling the API and the vendor implemented API.

issue_title: Extract submenus into a new project
issue_body:
The submenu feature is nice, but it's very complex. What if we extracted the submenu code into a new GitHub project at https://github.com/python-cmd2/cmd2-submenus, and packaged it in pypi as a separate wheel, something like cmd2-submenus. This would enable:

1. Easier maintenance of the core cmd2 module
2. Version dependency between releases of cmd2 and cmd2-submenus. If cmd2-submenus lags behind cmd2 features, users of both modules can make a decision whether they want to most current cmd2 capabilities, or submenu support.
3. Set a pattern for extensibility of cmd2 via additional packages instead of changes to the core.

I'm willing to take a crack at the extraction of the submenu code if there is interest in pursuing this proposal.

issue_body: For extra safety, the etag of a local file should not only contain the mtime, but also the filesize and inode number. I think all of those things are returned by `os.stat`. The modification needs to happen in `vdirsyncer.utils.get_etag_from_file`.

issue_title: Tweak output to not trigger markdown heading status
issue_body:
We have a lot of horizontal lines of dashes in the Certbot output, e.g.

```
Which names would you like to activate HTTPS for?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1: example.com
2: www.example.com
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

Unfortunately, Markdown interprets any text above one of these lines as a heading, leading to distracting and weird formatting when someone pastes it into the forum:

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/certbot-apache-succeeded-now-site-times-out/63168/3

Arguably they should wrap things in triple-backticks, but this happens quite a lot: People who need help on the forum often don't take time to learn about Markdown. It would be nice to make a small tweak so that formatting just works correctly. Unfortunately, lines of underscores, plus signs, colons, or equal signs all have the same effect. One simple tweak that works is inserting a space (or multiple spaces) in the line of hyphens:

- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------

or

- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -

issue_title: "SERVFAIL looking up A for..." for server in mainland China, Aliyun, DNSpod
issue_labels: [{'id': 277074172, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20acme', 'name': 'area: acme', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 596606268, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20dns', 'name': 'area: dns', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 146954790, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '159818', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version): Ubuntu Trusty
## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc): OS package manager (ppa)
## I ran this command and it produced this output: sudo certbot --apache

 - The following errors were reported by the server:

   Domain: qa1.zaomengshe.com
   Type:   connection
   Detail: DNS problem: SERVFAIL looking up A for qa1.zaomengshe.com

There seem to be similar problems reported in https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/1610, but that appeared to be closed inconclusively.  The problems are to do with the server being in mainland China, and using DNSpod as a DNS provider.

This is the latest of several attempts I've made to use certbot.  The server concerned is a test website, but is under the TLD zaomengshe.com same as the live site.  The live site has a Comodo certificate.  The test server runs ten different domains and I like to add new ones, so using commercial cert providers is more trouble than it's worth.

I've been irregularly trying to set up LetsEncrypt on this domain for about a year.  Every time there have been DNS problems.  Clearly the suggestions from certbot are not the problem: the site is up and running.

Rather, previously the problem has always been the DNS provider and the fact that both the DNS provider and the server are in mainland China behind the GFW.  Switching to a DNS provider outside mainland China fixes the problem.  However, 99% of our users are inside mainland China.  I can't take the risk of using a non-Chinese DNS provider.

Perhaps certbot just doesn't work for sites in the Chinese mainland?  That wouldn't surprise me.

I have no idea what is happening behind the scenes.  When it says "The server could not connect to the client to verify the domain", I have no idea who is the server and who is the client, or what sort of connection it's trying to make.  I suspect that there's something wrong with LetsEncrypt's own servers that makes them unable to contact sites registered with DNSpod.  If someone can point me to documentation explaining what's really happening, that would help as well.

Thanks!

issue_title: [Feature] Add variable board to SConstruct
issue_body:
The variable board will be used in the SConstruct file for building the verilog files for the specified board
Ex:
scons board=icestick

Current board values will be:  icestick, icezum, go-board

If the variable board is set, it will override other fpga flags passed:

Ex:
scons board=icezum fpga_size=8k

It won't set the fpga_size to 8k, because it will use the one determined for the icezum board (1k)

```python

issue_title: exception_handler provides inconsistent API
issue_body:
`SalesforceGeneralError` and its subclasses all accept `content`. For those users who are familiar with Requests, the expectation here would be that `exc.content` would be bytes. However, `simple_salesforce.util.exception_handler` generates exceptions that have either a content attribute that is parsed JSON or text (unicode). Further prior usage of `SalesforceGeneralError` directly was returning providing `response.content` from Requests as the `content` which was `bytes`. So there are 3 cases users must handle.

I suggest that `exc.content` be one consistent type (text or bytes) and that a convenience method that effectively proxies to `response.json()`. This implies either storing the response on the exception objects or some of the headers.

issue_title: remove check for task.dependencies in cot.verify
issue_body:
We're likely going to need to block tasks via dummy tasks at some point, likely as soon as pushapk cot verification.  We likely only need to verify the current task, its decision task, and `upstreamArtifacts` and their decision/`upstreamArtifacts` tasks up to the tree.  This means we can ignore `task.dependencies`.

This also means we'll need to make sure all *scripts do not interact with artifacts that are downloaded outside of `upstreamArtifacts`, unless they do their own validation.

issue_title: Ensure interoperability with Lightning etc.
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Different trustline / state channel / payment channel based networks use different hashlocks, which makes trustless payments across them impossible.

Lightning: SHA-256d¹ (two iterations of SHA-256), 32-byte preimage
Interledger: SHA-256, 32-byte preimage
Raiden: Ethereum-SHA-3, 32-byte preimage

In terms of the underlying blockchains, our choices are:

Bitcoin: SHA-256d¹, <=520 byte preimage
Ethereum: Any hash algorithm (smart contracts), no preimage size restriction, though 32 bytes (uint256) is convenient
Ripple ([PayChan](https://ripple.com/build/amendments/#paychan)): [Crypto-conditions](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-thomas-crypto-conditions-02) (SHA-256, Ed25519, RSA, Compound Conditions), no preimage size restriction

¹ Note that SHA-256d(x) = SHA-256(SHA-256(x)), so I can trivially prove that a SHA-256 condition C is fulfilled by the same secret as a given SHA-256d condition C' by showing C' = SHA-256(C). This has the effect that networks that use SHA-256 and SHA-256d are mutually interoperable with the security level of SHA-256.

## Solution

Raiden should support SHA-256 (instead of or in addition² to SHA-3) or an alternative to Raiden with SHA-256 support should be created for inter-chain/inter-ledger capability.

² We don't need all networks' condition types to be exactly the same, we just need some lowest common denominator that is shared between all of them.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Decide whether SHA-256 should be added as (a) an alternative to SHA-3 in Raiden, (b) a replacement to SHA-3 in Raiden or (c) a fork of Raiden
- [ ] Define a data format for the new SHA-256 lock
- [ ] Implement and test the SHA-256 lock

issue_title: Use zeroMQ for multiprocessing
issue_body:
The SoS executor is very complex now with a lot of message passing via pipes. Pipe objects are passed around so a process might have a pipe for its parent and a pipe for its worker, and so on. Whereas this has been working all right after hours of debugging, the code is messy.

It appears that zeroMQ has been used in such scenarios so if we are adding anything major to the executor, perhaps we can use zeroMQ for message passing, at least [has better performance than pipe](https://taotetek.net/2011/02/03/python-multiprocessing-zeromq-vs-queue/). Also, having a process dedicated to signature etc might also be a good idea. That it so say, we collect all signatures in a single file or database, start a process to read it all, and perform signature validation etc on request.

issue_title: Field.build_vocab breaks when using dict fields to load CSV
issue_body:
Hi!

Using the master version of this repo, I tried to create a `TabularDataset` using a `dict` to load specific columns from a CSV (#217). While the `TabularDataset` object was initialised fine (unlike the PyPI version, where using a dict for fields ends in a crash), trying to build vocab using those fields does not in fact build the vocab.

Example:

```
sentence = data.Field(lower=True, eos_token='<END>',
                    include_lengths=True, batch_first=True,
                    tokenize=word_tokenize)
fields = {'sentence': ('sentence', sentence), 
              ... }  # some other fields here 
train_data = data.TabularDataset(path=str(train_path), format='csv',
                             fields=fields, skip_header=False)
sentence.build_vocab(train_data, max_size=80000,
                     vectors=GloVe(name='6B', dim=100))  # doesn't actually build the vocab
```

The resulting vocab only has the 3 tokens UNK, PAD, and EOS, and nothing else.

I have found other bugs on the master version, so perhaps this functionality is still in progress and I shouldn't attempt to use it yet?

issue_title: Make `DjangoUserMixin.get_user` return only active users
issue_body:
If a user is already logged in, Django's default backend doesn't authenticate the user if it has been inactive.
Social auth is not considering this, and validates any inactive user if they are already logged in.

issue_title: Crispy filter does not include form media after 1.6.0 release
issue_body:
The crispy filter does not include a form's media anymore since release 1.6.0. This appears to be a bug as in the previous release, 1.5.2, the crispy filter when applied to a form or formset's context variable would include the form's media when rendered. **In the template** ``` {% load crispy_forms_filters %} ... {{ form|crispy }} ``` **Form class** ``` class MyForm(forms.Form): date=forms.DateField( label='Approved', required=True, widget=DatePlaceholder, ) ... ``` The date field uses a widget that requires some javaScript and CSS to work correctly. These files are included in the form's media when constructed in the view. In version 1.5.2, this media would be included in the rendered html as seen below: ```
``` However, when using version 1.6.0, without changing any code, this is the rendered html: ```
``` As you can see, the form media is no longer being included when being rendered by the filter. This seems like it is a bug introduced between release 1.5.2 and 1.6.0. It initially seems like this could be related to the changes from #525 with the optional inclusion of form media when using the `crispy` template tag. Perhaps the include_media context variable is not being set correctly.

issue_title: Native client join waits for commands sequentially
issue_body:
Currently the native parallel client will wait for command completion and check for EOF of remote commands sequentially in the main thread, using one greenlet.

This can leave some hosts that have finished in a non-EOF state where subsequent `get_exit_codes` calls will not gather their exit codes.

Re-write `join` for the native parallel client so that it can:

a) Wait for EOF in parallel
b) Gather exit codes for all hosts that have finished
c) Raise `Timeout` for any hosts that have not when timeout is set

This can possibly be a new function that does not require an output object, to enable simpler code without an output variable like:

```
client.run_command(<..>)
client.join_last()
```

issue_title: How to write it: RPC client with out of order responses
issue_body:
This is a fun little problem and I was wondering what is the best way to use trio to solve it. I hope this is appropriate here.

Consider a simple RPC protocol:

Example request: `{"id": 15353, "params": ...}`

Example successful response: `{"id": 15353, "result": ...}`

Example error response: `{"id": 15353, "error": ...}`

The client can send multiple requests concurrently. The server can respond out of order.

Suppose we want to write a client for this protocol using trio. We want to send many concurrent requests and handle their responses. We want to have a synchronous-looking API, e.g. `try { response = await send(request) } except { ... }`.

---

Here is how I would solve it (I've hidden it in case someone wants to come up with an independent solution 💭):

<details>

- Add a future/promise abstraction using `trio.Event` internally. Curio has a [simple one](https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/blob/bd0e2cb7741278d1d9288780127dc0807b1aa5b1/curio/promise.py).

- The sending function creates a future, stores it in a `request id -> future` dict, sends the request, and waits on the future.

- Spawn a received task which receives responses from the server and sets the result/exception on the corresponding future in the `request id -> future` dict.

- For each request, spawn a task which would use the sending function.

</details>

Is there a more elegant way?

issue_title: Introduce performance measurements
issue_body:
Obligatory background: we all agree (or at least nodded our heads in agreement when this was mentioned many times in the past) that Trio has a number of priorities over speed of execution. Optimizing for speed or any other performance metric should never hinder simplicity, "humanness", correctness, maintainability, predictability, security, debuggability, or other design principles. Premature optimization _might_ be the root of _some_ evil, and focusing on efficiency carries the risk of getting in the way of innovation. Even just identifying what is performance critical takes a lot of effort and is a moving target.

Given all of this, I believe we should **provide some tools to make sure that Trio doesn't add too much overhead and allows efficient user code to run efficiently**. This is more complicated than "create a bunch of benchmarks" but I want to start by creating a bunch of benchmarks, at least to start thinking about this. I had found myself caring about performance in a few cases and I want to do more work in that direction. It's relevant to me and it might be to someone else so it would be nice to share.

When I say a bunch of benchmarks I think of something that should reproduce a realistic workload for a Trio application. I don't think we are in a position to actually understand what a "realistic workload" is. I have a couple examples that I can derive from things I do for my paid job, and we can think of some more more (including something from projects you are working on, porting asyncio benchmarks and examples, adding trio-asyncio examples, etc). We can also produce some microbenchmarks. Also "performance" is a broad term that can mean many different things. In an I/O application it can mean throughput, latency, bandwidth usage, fairness, CPU impact, memory impact, or anything I did not think about. Covering all of these aspects can be hard (although sometimes some is a good proxy for others, e.g. a microbenchmark for execution time of some operation may provide hints about CPU, latency and throughput).

In terms of process, I don't think that performance measurements should get in the way of approving a PR or anything like that. Yeah, it can be nice to have comparisons but, really, I don't think that we are anywhere close to ready to make performance part of the development process. Benchmarks are lies that can have a mild informative value that is often hard to interpret. Still, like a good test suite, a benchmark suite can sometimes make it obvious that something is a regression, or an improvement, and it can be improved over time to provide better coverage of real-world use cases.

Anything we can learn from other projects? Tools (benchmark runners and the such) we can borrow? General feedback?

@njsmith would you see the `trio` repo as the space for this or would it better live in a different project?

issue_title: Requirements are inconsistent
issue_body:
In recent versions of localstack, the pinned versions of the requirements are in conflict. Specifically, moto-ext==1.1.24 requires botocore>=1.7.12,  and boto3==1.4.4 requires botocore<1.6.0,>=1.5.0.

This prevents localstack from being installed by tools which do proper dependency resolution, such as pipenv. This issue appears to have been introduced version 0.8.2.

To reproduce (assuming [pipenv](https://docs.pipenv.org/install.html#installing-pipenv) is installed):
`mkdir repro` 
`pipenv install localstack`

issue_title: No logs when using "docker-reuse" as LAMBDA_EXECUTOR
issue_body:
Firstly, great job on localstack! 👌

Using this product for first time, and making use of "docker-reuse" as the LAMBDA_EXECUTOR, for performance reasons. It's proving difficult to debug the lambda when things go wrong, as seemingly there has been no output.

This [thread](https://github.com/localstack/localstack/issues/117) suggested using plain old `docker logs`, however every time I've done that - the logs appear empty.

Then I realised, the command ran in the above example was nodejs, where as my output displays "/bin/bash". I assume this is a key difference between running with "docker-reuse" and just "docker".

```
CONTAINER ID        IMAGE                           COMMAND                  CREATED                  STATUS                  PORTS                                                                             NAMES
eeee99282d09        lambci/lambda:nodejs8.10        "/bin/bash"              Less than a second ago   Up Less than a second                                                                                     localstack_lambda_arn_aws_lambda_us-east-1_000000000000_function_my-test-lambda
```

Changing our setup back to using "docker" as the LAMBDA_EXECUTOR allows us to use `docker logs`.

Is this a bug? If not, should a workaround (if known) be documented?

Error extracting weights from tensorproto.
issue_body:
The `dims` attribute of `TensorProto` defined a list of the dimensions the array has to be reshaped into. However, in the Shuffenet model, TensorProto objects are similar to :
`TensorProto([5], 7, #undef, Float32[], Int32[], Array{UInt8,1}[], Int64[], "OC2_DUMMY_5", "", UInt8[0x01, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x04, 0x00  …  0x00, 0x00, 0x1c, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00], Float64[], UInt64[])`

As we can see, the `dims` are `[5]` while the length of the array data is 40. Clearly this cannot be reshaped to match the dims.

Why is this happening and how should the data be reshaped in this case?

issue_title: ResNet50 and MobileNet performances on ImageNet
issue_body:
I have been checked all the models here with TensorFlow backend (see the [commit](https://github.com/keras-team/keras-applications/commit/26c1c11cdf8b2f9b5c3e281c538c1b0fe1a352b3)). For ImageNet validation set, all except ResNet50 and MobileNet showed the same top-k errors with [TensorNets](https://github.com/taehoonlee/tensornets#performances).
- ResNet50: a slight difference (25.296 / 7.980 / 6.852 vs. 25.126 / 7.982 / 6.842).
- MobileNet: a significant difference (34.180 / 13.858 / 10.798 vs. 29.576 / 10.496 / 8.774 for MobileNet(alpha=1.0)).

We need to investigate where the differences come from 🔥.

I noticed that in dqn.compile() the optimizer to compile self.model is always 'sgd'.
I understand that for self.target_model the optimizer doesn't matter, but I don't understand why this should be the case for self.model.

`        # We never train the target model, hence we can set the optimizer and loss arbitrarily.
        self.target_model = clone_model(self.model, self.custom_model_objects)
        self.target_model.compile(optimizer='sgd', loss='mse')    # I understand this                      
        self.model.compile(optimizer='sgd', loss='mse')               # But not this
`

However, I did actually change the last line and did not see any difference when running one of my models. So I guess the question is really why does the optimizer that is passed to self.model.compile() not have any influence on the training ??

Thanx,
    Axel

issue_title: Many scripts do use journalctl output in live mode which takes time
issue_body:
**What are you missing in citellus?**

As journalctl in offline mode is defined based on sosreport, for 'live' mode generate once to a temporary file, prefill on first execution or if empty, and then handle that file as journalctl variable like what we do in sosreport mode but for live mode.

This will require to cleanup some of the scripts that use it and use the var instead but will be faster in live exection

issue_title: Can't build against RHEL cfitsio
issue_body:
Redhat Enterprise Linux (and derived distributions like Fedora) install libcfitsio.a in /usr/lib/ and /usr/lib64, but place fitsio.h in /usr/include/cfitsio/. The current build system expects a common root directory for include/fitsio.h and lib/libcfitsio.a.

Should scons be more flexible about this or should we just ignore this since we want to get rid of cfitsio anyway?
